To ask the Minister of Health to detail any alternative provision that will be put in place for children with complex needs who would have attended special school summer schemes this year.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of Summer Scheme provision in Special Schools to the children attending them and their families. To ensure that the schemes can proceed this summer, and that no such alternative provision is therefore required, I have tasked the Chief Nursing Officer to work with nursing leaders across the five regional HSC Trusts to develop plans to meet the Education Authority’s request for a registered nurse to be present at each summer scheme. Whilst extremely challenging, my Department is committed to making every effort to source this identified additional nursing cover, involving appropriate staff with the right skills and knowledge including the appropriate children’s safeguarding training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when he was first made aware that a lack of nursing provision could prevent special school summer schemes from taking place in 2026.
answer_text: Concerns over the delivery of SEN summer schemes were first brought to my attention at a joint meeting with the National Association of Head Teachers (NAHT) on 3 February 2026, which I attended along with the Minister of Education.  Among other issues discussed at that meeting, concerns were raised about the impact on Principals of ensuring safe delivery of the summer schemes; this did not however include any suggestion that a nurse would be required on each site.On 2 March 2026, my officials met with the Education Authority (EA) and at that point were advised that in the EA’s view a nurse was required on each summer scheme site to provide oversight and healthcare interventions for children with complex needs in those settings. This level of nursing provision has not been in place in previous years and indeed is currently not the case during term time for many of the children who will be attending summer schemes.While this will be challenging, my Department is committed to making every effort to source the identified additional nursing cover, involving appropriate staff with the right skills and knowledge while also including the appropriate children’s safeguarding training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he took to secure the healthcare support required to enable special school summer schemes to proceed this summer.
answer_text: Appropriate nursing support has been provided to support summer schemes in Special Schools for many years, with a number of different models of delivery operating depending on the identified need. Not all children attending these schemes require nursing support, but around 20% of children attending SEN schools with complex health care needs require a higher level of support with healthcare interventions.In early March my officials met with the Education Authority (EA) and Department of Education (DE) and were advised that in the EA’s view a nurse was required on each summer scheme site to provide oversight and healthcare interventions for children with complex needs in those settings. This level of nursing provision has not been in place in all summer schemes in previous years and indeed is currently not the case during term time for many of the children who will be attending summer schemes. Onsite nursing support for all SEN Summer Schemes is not considered to be necessary by my Department’s Chief Nursing Officer, nor by senior nurse leaders within HSCTs.  To allow the continuation of the SEN Summer Schemes my officials engaged with DE and EA to explore appropriate nursing cover options. Those options provided an enhanced level of support which included additional onsite training for classroom assistants and also direct telephone access to a registered Community Children’s Nurse familiar with that cohort of children during the school/scheme day as required. EA remained of the view that, in the absence of their desired resolution, they would have no option but to cancel the schemes for summer 2026 on the grounds of safety and proceeded to make an announcement of the cancellation of the Summer Schemes on 25 March 2026.Recognising the importance of these Summer Schemes to the children attending them and their families, I have tasked the Chief Nursing Officer to work with the five regional HSC Trust nursing leaders to develop plans to meet the request of a registered nurse on each site to ensure the summer schemes can progress this year. Whilst extremely challenging, my Department is committed to making every effort to source the identified additional nursing cover, involving appropriate staff with the right skills and knowledge including the appropriate children’s safeguarding training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the Warm Healthy Homes Strategy, to detail (i) any action he has taken to ensure that all customers on the Medical Care Register are placed on the most advantageous tariff available; (ii) any measures that will be introduced to ensure that this voluntary register captures all those who are eligible; and (iii) whether he will consider cross referencing this register with other official datasets such as Health and Social Care Trust lists of patients prescribed medical devices, and those receiving benefits under the Special Rules for Terminal Il
answer_text: The Warm Healthy Homes Strategy is an Executive strategy with responsibility shared across a number of Departments and Arm’s Length Bodies. It is a ten-year strategy including 33 strategic actions. My officials are currently progressing work on the development of the inaugural Warm Healthy Homes Strategy Action Plan. This will set out agreed timelines, responsibilities and delivery arrangements across Departments and relevant partners for the first phase of the Strategy. We will work with delivery partners to develop measures to gain an understanding of the impact that our actions are having as the strategy is implemented. Strategic actions in relation to the Medical Register and the development of data sharing will be fully considered in the development of action plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any mandatory training doctors undertaking postgraduate training or professional development must complete.
answer_text: All Health and Social Care (HSC) employees, including resident doctors in postgraduate training, are required to complete the below mandatory training modules: Equality, Diversity & InclusivityManual HandlingFire SafetyInformation Governance, including GDPRCyber Security AwarenessFraud AwarenessIn addition to these HSCwide modules, Trusts have also identified Mandatory Training and Induction modules to meet clinical governance requirements. This list is not exhaustive, and individual Trusts may identify additional requirements. Agreed modules include:Safe HandoverIRMER (Ionising Radiation (Medical Exposure) Regulations)ConsentDeath CertificationContacting the CoronerSafe Insulin PrescribingInfection ControlChild ProtectionHyponatraemiaMental Capacity Act TrainingRight Patient, Right BloodNational Early Warning Score (NEWS)ResuscitationEach Royal College or Faculty can also determine additional specialty-specific courses or training requirements, which are incorporated into the relevant GMCapproved curricula.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children waiting to access speech and language therapy, broken down by those waiting longer than 13 weeks, further detailed in six-month time bands; and (ii) the number of children who have been removed from the initial waiting list and are waiting for therapy on a secondary waiting list.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why (i) occupational therapy; and (ii) speech and language therapy, waiting list data is not routinely disaggregated between children and adults.
answer_text: Waiting times for occupational therapy and speech and language therapy are routinely collected in accordance with the monitoring arrangements set out under the Ministerial Access Target for allied health professionals (AHPs). The target specifies that patients should wait no longer than 13 weeks from referral to the commencement of treatment by an AHP.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have been working collaboratively to develop enhanced reporting that enables the disaggregation of total waiting list figures between adults and children. With all HSC Trusts now having fully implemented the encompass system, the metric required to differentiate between adult and paediatric pathways within AHP waiting list data has been developed. This metric is currently undergoing evaluation and validation within each Trust to ensure accuracy and consistency. Subject to the successful completion of this validation process, it is anticipated that disaggregated adult and children’s AHP waiting list data will be available for formal reporting later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to strengthen early detection, diagnosis, and research into lobular breast cancer, given its increasing prevalence and the evidence that it behaves differently from, and requires different treatment approaches than, other breast cancer types.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people who have been diagnosed with lobular breast cancer, in the last five years; and (ii) the proportion of all breast cancer diagnoses this represents.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR), based at Queen’s University Belfast and funded by the Public Health Agency, produces the Official Statistics on cancer incidence, prevalence and survival in Northern Ireland. NICR publications are the authoritative source for female breast cancer incidence figures. The most recent validated data cover cancer diagnoses up to and including 2022, as set out in the Breast Cancer Official Statistics Report (2024). These data and associated factsheets are available at: www.qub.ac.uk/research-centres/nicr/The most recent validated data from NICR shows that between 2018 and 2022, there were 7,566 breast cancer cases in Northern Ireland. Of these, 1,035 women were diagnosed with invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC), representing approximately 13.7% of all female breast cancer diagnoses during this period.My Department recognises that ILC has distinct clinical characteristics, including a more subtle radiological appearance and a tendency for diffuse tumour spread.There are a number of health and social care system activities that contribute to early detection, diagnosis and understanding of all breast cancer subtypes, including ILC. These include the ongoing development of screening and diagnostic services, collaboration with clinical and system partners, and the use of populationlevel data to inform wider planning and research. These activities help ensure that the needs associated with different breast cancer subtypes are considered within wider cancer care initiatives.My Department will continue to work across the health and social care system, and with other relevant organisations, to support early detection and the effective diagnosis and treatment of all forms of breast cancer.

answer_text: No. (ii) 	I am currently considering proposals for Northern Ireland’s first Gambling Code of Practice, following detailed consideration of the consultation responses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware of (a) Public Health Wales November 2022 report Gambling Health Needs Assessment for Wales; and (b) the Public Health Scotland April 2025 report Healthcare Needs Assessment on Gambling Harm; and (ii) whether his Department has engaged with the Public Health Agency about conducting a health needs assessment for gambling harm.
answer_text: My Department is aware of both the Public Health Wales’s Gambling Health Needs Assessment, published in November 2022, and the Public Health Scotland’s Healthcare Needs Assessment on Gambling Harm, published in April 2025.Due to the budgetary pressure my Department is facing and as there is no dedicated funding source for gambling harms in Northern Ireland, unlike in Wales and Scotland who have access to revenue from the statutory levy on gambling operators in Great Britian, it has not been possible for my Department to engage with the Public Health Agency about conducting a health needs assessment for gambling harm in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any consideration he has given to introducing newborn screening for spinal muscular atrophy.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the anticipated timeline for the scoping exercises into (i) potential cross-border cooperation; and (ii) less invasive post-mortem approaches, in respect of paediatric pathology services.
answer_text: As I outlined in the recent Assembly debate, I am committed to doing everything possible to improve paediatric pathology services, including exploring the potential development of an allisland service and the use of lessinvasive postmortem approaches. Scoping work in both these areas has commenced. My conversations with my counterpart in the Republic of Ireland, while positive, have confirmed they do not currently have the capacity to undertake additional post-mortems from Northern Ireland. Their immediate priority is to build the necessary capacity that could support an all-island approach in the future through the training and retention of staff with appropriate specialist expertise. It is important to acknowledge that this is in the context of a global shortage of paediatric pathologists and is likely to take several years, and progress will depend on factors beyond our direct control. My Department is also exploring the feasibility of less invasive post-mortem techniques being safely and appropriately adopted in Northern Ireland which may provide clinical answers for some families and mitigate the need for transportation in the future, however these approaches remain at a relatively early stage of development internationally. My Department will continue to engage constructively with colleagues in the Republic of Ireland and in England to explore every possible option for improving these services for families at this most difficult time. However, it is not possible to be definitive on timescales at this stage as progress will depend on external workforce and system-wide factors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of the study budget for doctors undertaking postgraduate training or professional development that must be used to pay for mandatory training.
answer_text: Mandatory training costs for medical specialty trainees in Northern Ireland are included within the overall available study budget of £1,250.  Where a trainee’s mandatory training costs exceed the £1,250 study budget in any given training year, the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency will meet these costs; however, this will result in a corresponding reduction in the funding available to that trainee for nonmandatory courses in the remaining years of their programme. Trainees in affected specialties are advised of this at the outset of their training programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the Neurology Delivery Team will be established, to implement the recommendations of the Regional Review of Neurology Services; (ii) who will lead this process; and (ii) any key stakeholders that will be involved.
answer_text: I recognise that the challenges within current neurology services are complex and wide ranging. In the context of these challenges, my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology report proposes the establishment of a Neurology Delivery Team (NDT) to drive forward implementation of the Review’s recommendations. To that end, my officials are currently identifying next steps regarding the establishment of the NDT. In addition, the analysis of responses to the public consultation on the Review report is nearing completion. I look forward to receiving the Consultation Analysis Report and next steps regarding implementation in the coming weeks. The NDT will be led by my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), supported by a dedicated clinical lead. It is vital that membership of the NDT incorporates a broad range of stakeholders including Health and Social Care Trusts, providers of services and professional bodies and the voluntary and community sector. It is vital that the voice of people with neurological conditions is also represented. I must be clear that full implementation of the recommendations proposed will require significant additional funding. While the NDT will be tasked with the establishment of an implementation plan as an initial priority, the scale and pace of implementation may have to be adjusted in line with available funding.In the interim, my officials continue to actively consider ways to drive improvements in services using current resources through collaborative approaches and innovative ways of working.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been made of barriers to accessing cancer treatment faced by children and young people due to travel costs, including any data on missed or delayed appointments attributable to financial constraints.
answer_text: The Department does not currently collect centralised data on the travel distances or travel-related costs incurred by children and young people under the age of 25, or by their families, when accessing cancer treatment. While individual Health and Social Care Trusts may hold operational information relating to patient transport arrangements, this is not collated at regional level in a manner that would allow reporting on travel patterns or associated financial impacts for this patient group.The Department has not undertaken a formal assessment specifically focused on financial barriers to accessing cancer treatment for children and young people, including those arising from travel costs. Data on missed or delayed appointments is collected by Trusts; however, the reasons for non-attendance are not routinely captured in a way that would allow identification of cases attributable to financial pressures or travel-related constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement taken place with third sector organisations in progressing the reconfiguration of adolescent and young adult cancer services, as outlined in the Cancer Strategy 2022-2032.
answer_text: Throughout the development and implementation of regional standards of care for adolescents and young adults (AYA) with cancer, my Department has actively engaged with a range of third sector organisations who provide services for children and young adults with cancer. This engagement has included participation in stakeholder workshops, consultation meetings, membership of task and finish groups, and ongoing collaboration.The Regional Standards of Care for AYA with cancer have been co-produced with healthcare professionals, partner organisations, and young people affected by cancer and their families. These organisations have provided valuable input on service design, patient experience, and support pathways.My Department will continue this collaborative approach as implementation progresses, maintaining strong partnerships with third-sector organisations to ensure that services for AYA with cancer meet the needs of those who use them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any data available on (i) diagnostic interval or time to diagnosis from first healthcare presentation, (ii) the number of times children and young people present to a healthcare service before receiving a diagnosis; and (iii) routes to diagnosis, for children and young people under 25 with cancer.
answer_text: (i) The Department does not hold standardised regional data on diagnostic intervals, number of healthcare presentations prior to diagnosis, or routes to diagnosis for children and young people under 25 with cancer. While elements of this information may exist within individual clinical records or have been collected for specific audit or research purposes, it is not compiled or reported system-wide.  The following information is available via research publication and Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) data which provides insight into diagnostic pathways for children and young people in Northern Ireland:Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children as a primary paediatric treatment centre participated in a UK wide prospective observational study recently published in the Lancet Journal in July 2025. The study; Quantifying diagnostic intervals and routes to diagnosis for children and young people with cancer in the UK (Lancet Regional Health–Europe, 2025) collected data on 1,957 children (0–18 years), including 51 from Northern Ireland (approximately 3% of the cohort).The primary outcome measure was the Total Diagnostic Interval (TDI), defined as time between symptom onset and diagnosis. The secondary outcome measures are the Patient Interval (PI), calculated as the interval between symptom onset and first presentation to healthcare, and the Diagnostic Interval (DI), time between first presentation and diagnosis.  Northern Ireland’s figures were not disaggregated in the published paper, however supplementary data indicates that there was no UK regional difference observed in the TDI with a median of 4.6 weeks.  Regional differences were observed in PI and DI.  The PI was shorter in England, Wales and Northern Ireland (median 1.0 weeks) compared to Scotland (2.0 weeks). Conversely, the DI was longer in England and Wales, with a median of 1.9 weeks compared to 1.0 weeks in Scotland and 0.4 week in NI. https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanepe/article/PIIS2666-7762(25)00121-8/fulltext(ii) The number of presentations to a healthcare service before receiving a diagnosis and the routes to diagnosis were not presented by region however, the study observed about three-quarters (1438, 74%) of CYP had 1–3 Health Care professional (HCP) visits before diagnosis. Two-thirds (1312, 67%) were diagnosed via emergency presentation, including attendance at the emergency department, emergency referral, transfer, or admission.(iii) In April 2025, the NI Cancer Registry (NICR) published an extensive paper on ‘Routes to Diagnosis’ for cancer patients diagnosed in 2018-2021. This release, funded by the Department of Health, provides details of the key event in each cancer patient’s pathway that most directly led to their cancer diagnosis.  This publication does not present findings by age groups however, NICR has supplied the relevant data as follows.Route to diagnosis for children and young adults diagnosed with cancer (ex NMSC) in 2018-2021Route to diagnosisAges 0 to 1

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the next Cancer Patient Experience Survey will be conducted; and (ii) whether any consideration has been given to developing a methodology that separately captures the experiences of children under 16 with cancer and their families.
answer_text: when the next Cancer Patient Experience Survey will be conducted.Northern Ireland participated in the National Cancer Patient Experience Survey (CPES) in 2015 and again in 2018. CPES is designed to capture comprehensive feedback on patients’ experiences of cancer care, from diagnosis through to discharge from treatment. The survey provides valuable insights to identify areas requiring service improvement, monitor progress over time, and enable benchmarking with the rest of the UK.Ideally, Northern Ireland would resume participation in the National Cancer Patient Experience Survey (CPES). However, the current cost of participation is substantial and cannot be justified within the present financial context, particularly given the significant number of high-priority clinical areas requiring fundingTherefore, to address this Cancer Services in all trusts have collaborated regionally to design and pilot a new patient survey mechanism, incorporating questions aligned to similar domains used in CPES. These are: Support at diagnosisExplanation & understanding of diagnosisInformation about cancer typeAwareness of Multi-disciplinary meetingInvolvement & explanation of treatmentOutpatient appointmentsHolistic needs assessmentClinical nurse specialist and Drs Overall treatment The data can be analysed and presented by tumour type and by Trust. This will support both Trusts and cancer multidisciplinary teams to develop their own targeted improvement plans, as well as contribute to the development of regional improvement priorities. (ii) whether any consideration has been given to developing a methodology that separately captures the experiences of children under 16 with cancer and their familiesThere are currently no specific cancer patient surveys in place for children and their families within the Children’s and Young People’s Cancer and Haematology Service. However, Belfast Trust has confirmed that discussions have begun with its Audit Team, who lead on this work, and further engagement is planned to agree the rollout of a patient survey within this specialty.The Royal Belfast Hospital Sick Children service does, however facilitate a family focus group that meets three to four times per year. This group was initially established by the Young Lives social worker and includes families across all age groups. Its aim is to gather feedback on the service and support ongoing service improvement. Plans are in place to broaden participation by involving the Trust’s Patient Involvement Team, enabling engagement with a wider range of families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions being taken to improve earlier detection and ensure timely referral pathways for suspected lung cancer.
answer_text: My department recognises the importance of the need to improve early detection and timely referral pathways for suspected lung cancer which will requires a whole system approach.I have committed as part of the three-year Health and Social Care Plan, that by April 2027, and subject to additional funding, to have a new targeted lung screening programme in Northern Ireland. The lung screening programme will be a very significant service development, encompassing new screening services and a range of additional diagnostic and treatment services. This will require input and collaboration from multiple partners. My Department continues to work with colleagues across Northern Ireland and the rest of the UK to learn lessons, to establish the resources required and determine a likely timeline to implement the programme. To improve public knowledge of signs and symptoms of lung cancer, a public awareness campaign was launched.  “Spotting lung cancer early”, ran in Spring 2024, coordinated by Cancer Research UK, the Public Health Agency, and the Northern Ireland Cancer Network.  This targeted initiative increased public awareness of signs and symptoms and urged individuals to see their GP promptly. As part of its 2025–26 workplan, the NI Cancer Network (NICaN) Lung Cancer Clinical Reference Group will review the Primary Care Suspected Cancer Referral Guidelines for Lung Cancer in collaboration with primary care colleagues. This will be published and supported by a GP/Primary Care Education session. Two Rapid Diagnostic Centres (RDCs) at South Tyrone Hospital and Whiteabbey Hospital have been established to fast-track patients with vague, concerning symptoms who do not necessarily meet suspect lung cancer 'red flag' referral criteria.  Additional imaging capacity has been ringfenced at RDCs to address regional red flag/urgent/time critical imaging waiting lists which will benefit the lung cancer diagnostic pathway.The Regional Medical Imaging Board (RMIB) has formed an Artificial Intelligence sub-group to coordinate regional adoption of AI in diagnostic imaging and to recommend future implementation. A draft AI strategy for imaging services is awaiting approval, aiming to use AI as an adjunct to clinical care to improve efficiency and patient outcomes. The subgroup has evaluated AI software for Chest X-Ray reporting, and the Southern Health and Social Care Trust will pilot this software in the coming months.By combining expanded diagnostic services, public symptom awareness, and targeted screening efforts, my Department is aiming to diagnose more lung cancers early rather than via emergency routes, optimising survival outcomes and reducing late-stage presentations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to all published cancer waiting time targets, to detail the amount that relate specifically to suspected lung cancer.
answer_text: The most recent official statistics for suspected lung cancer referrals were published on 2 October 2025 referring to the quarter end of June 2025. Please refer to Northern Ireland waiting time statistics: cancer waiting times April - June 2025 | Department of Health .  31-day target states that at least 98% of patients urgently referred with a suspected cancer should begin their first definitive treatment within 31 days of a decision to treat.  Data for quarter to end of June 2025 shows that 353 lung cancer patients were treated and of those 90.1% of patients met the 31-day target.The 62-day Target states that at least 95% of patients urgently referred by a GP with suspected cancer should begin their first definitive treatment within 62 days of referral, 46% of lung cancer patients met this target in the data for quarter to end of June 2025.  The next issue of the Cancer Waiting Time Statistics bulletin will have a reporting period of 1 July to 30 September 2025 and will be published on 15 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to conduct a primary evidence review of gambling-related harms for the population.
answer_text: It is well documented that Northern Ireland currently has a deficit in up-to-date data and evidence on gambling harms. To address this, my Department has worked collaboratively with cross-departmental colleagues to strengthen the evidence base. This includes the inclusion of gambling-related questions in the Young Person’s Behaviour and Attitude Survey 2022 (YPBAS) and, for the first time, in a pilot module, in the Northern Ireland Health Survey 2023/24.  The results from the YPBAS were published on 6 December 2023 and the Northern Ireland Health Survey pilot results were published on 11 December 2025.   In addition, the Department for Communities commissioned a Gambling Prevalence Survey during 2024, with results published earlier this year. Whilst there are no plans to conduct a formal primary evidence review, my officials will continue to work with officials in the Department for Communities to identify the most appropriate source of gambling prevalence and harm information for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce screening programmes across all health and social care services to identify and assist people with gambling problems.
answer_text: In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission bespoke gambling treatment services in the future would need to be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, to create a fuller understanding of the population need in Northern Ireland and in the context of future budget availability.  Funding for services aimed at prevention and treatment of gambling harm would require new or additional funding.In order to develop a fuller understanding of the population need in Northern Ireland, my Department is working collaboratively with officials in the Department for Communities to build an evidence base on the level of problem gambling in Northern Ireland and its associated harms.  Whilst there are currently no plans to introduce screening programmes in Northern Ireland to identify and assist people with gambling problems, my officials will consider the options for the implementation of recommendations in the All Party Group report on gambling, including screening, should funding become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average annual study budget available for resident doctors who are undertaking training or professional development and (ii) how this compares to what is available in (a) England; (b) Scotland; and (c) Wales.
answer_text: The study budget for medical specialty trainees in Northern Ireland is currently up to a maximum of £1250 per academic year. England, Scotland and Wales determine their own rates for the study support they provide to resident doctors who are undertaking training or professional development. As England and Scotland have multiple providers, rates vary and are not standard within those jurisdictions. In Wales, a revised study leave cap of £1,000 has recently been agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what specific measures are being taken to reduce neurology waiting lists.
answer_text: I acknowledge that the current waiting times for neurology are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.My Department’s regional review of neurology services sets the strategic direction to improve access to neurology services. There are a number of recommendations proposed in the final report, this includes growth in the workforce to provide additional capacity as well as an improved skill mix. This, in turn, will support new ways of working to ensure that access to outpatient appointments is prioritised for people who need to see a Consultant Neurologist, while also offering alternatives to outpatient appointments for those whose needs can be met in a different way.   Following the closure of the consultation of the regional review, my Department is finalising a consultation analysis report which will inform the development of a final action plan. I expect this work to be completed in the coming months. In the interim, my officials continue to actively consider ways to address lengthy waiting lists and to understand how current systems could be effectively used to enhance patient access through new, and innovative ways of working and collaborative approaches. The Elective Care Framework (ECF), published in May 2024 sets out a roadmap for reducing our waiting times over the next five years. £85 million of the ECF funding is primarily focused on red flag and time-critical patients. Unfortunately, current workforce limitations within the neurology specialty present challenges in creating additional short-term capacity.Despite the challenges, dedicated recurrent funding has been secured to fund a Specialist Neurology Nurse, strengthening efforts to reduce waiting times and enhance patient care.I must stress that reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix and will not be resolved in a year or two. It is, however, undoubtedly a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the percentage of the earmarked funding for tackling waiting lists that has been directed towards reducing neurology waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the policy to reduce electricity connection costs for rural homes and businesses.
answer_text: A review of grid connection costs has remained one of my top priorities.As such, I published my decision paper on fairer grid connection costs on 26 November, with implementation planned for 2026.My decision will remove unfairness and enable homes and businesses to connect to the grid at a fair price. It will deliver fairness and balance regardless of your location across the region, help to encourage new investment in low carbon technologies and renewables and it will place us on a more level playing field with our neighbours when it comes to attracting new business opportunities and renewable investment.Following this decision the Utility Regulator will work to modify NIE Networks’ licence and a new statement of connection charges will be published in the new year. Change will take effect in the first half of 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) children; and (ii) adults, living with an acquired brain injury, broken down by (a) traumatic brain injury; and (b) non-traumatic brain injury.
answer_text: It has not been possible to provide the specific data on the number of children and adults living with an acquired brain injury (ABI), broken down by traumatic and non-traumatic ABI. My Department and HSC Trusts do not hold this level of information.  The lack of detailed, localized data on ABI cases is due to a combination of factors related to data collection methodologies, standardisation issues, and the complex nature of the condition itself.It is estimated that there are approximately 2,000 people in Northern Ireland sustain a brain injury each year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any neuro rehabilitation services, available for children with an acquired brain injury.
answer_text: SPPG have contacted each of the Health and Social Care Trusts who have advised as follows; The Western HSC Trust advised that their Children with Disabilities service does not offer specialised support to children with acquired brain injury. Support for children in this cohort is however available through the Regional Acquired Brain Injury Consultation service.The Northern HSC Trust do not have a specific team for paediatric brain injury, children impacted by acquired brain injury would however be able to access Allied Health Professional, (AHP) services through referral to core services.The South Eastern HSC Trust advised that the Belfast Trust provide this service for children in South Eastern Trust area.The Southern HSC Trust advised these services are centralised and managed in Belfast. The Belfast HSC Trust advised that Children’s Services do not have a formal, commissioned paediatric neuro rehabilitation service for acquired brain injury. Children presenting to Belfast HSC Trust with an acquired brain injury will be under the care of a named Paediatrician who would be positioned to make referrals to inpatient and community services as clinically appropriate. The Belfast HSC Trust would also utilise Extra Contractual Referrals to healthcare providers across the United Kingdom for children diagnosed with acquired brain injuries where appropriate to do so.The Belfast HSC Regional Acquired Brain Injury Unit at Musgrave Park Hospital is an adult inpatient setting which offers individuals who are 16 years and over, a multidisciplinary led approach to rehabilitation following a brain injury. The multidisciplinary team consists of specialised Consultants and Nursing staff, who have a wealth of knowledge and experience in caring for patients with brain injuries. The team also work alongside Physiotherapists, Occupational Therapists, Speech & Language Therapists and Dietitians who form an accessible component in the patient’s rehabilitation journey. Families are included within the rehabilitation programme as far as possible. Individual patients are afforded an opportunity to work alongside an activity coordinator. This service provides a range of patient centered activities including, art therapy, music and games to enhance quality of life, build relationships with other patients and to enhance their confidence to develop a positive outlook.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department is considering any plans to bid to bring the Tour de France to the island of Ireland and for stages of the race to take place in the north.
answer_text: I am very supportive of hosting major events in the region, given the significant economic, media and social benefits they bring. My Tourism Vision and Action Plan sets out my ambition to develop a long-term programme of events for the North that will contribute to the delivery of my economic vision.	At present Tourism NI is developing a new Tourism Events Strategic Vision that will include consideration of internationally mobile events for which the north should bid to host and support. The criteria to inform future investment is yet to be established. Once this work is concluded, consideration will then be given to future major events to be bid for and hosted in the north, particularly in these challenging budgetary circumstances.	No approach has been made directly to the department in relation to future stages of the Tour de France.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the reasons for the delays in (a) cancer diagnosis; and (b) the time between diagnosis and decision to treat; and (ii) any actions being taken to address this.
answer_text: As referenced in response to AQW 35169, the date of cancer diagnosis is not currently recorded in cancer information systems, as existing access standards focus on measuring the time from referral or decision to treat, to first definitive treatment. These standards include the 62-day target (from referral to treatment) and the 31-day target (from decision to treat to first definitive treatment).  Information on the median waits against these targets by cancer site and Health and Social Care Trust has been provided in response to AQW 35169.The Department recognises that cancer waiting time performance remains significantly below target and acknowledges that this is unacceptable.  The 31-day performance (from decision to treat to first definitive treatment) remains relatively stable and much closer to target than both the 62-day and 14-day breast targets, indicating that once diagnosed, patients are generally treated promptly. The key challenge lies in timely diagnosis, which is critical to meeting the 62-day target.Diagnosis by day 28 is typically required to meet the 62-day target. This is directly dependent on timely access to first outpatient appointments, imaging and other diagnostic tests, all of which continue to face demand that exceeds available capacity.  Poor performance is driven by several interrelated factors, including workforce vacancies that limit the system’s responsiveness, reliance on non-recurrent funding, and the urgent need for sustained investment in core services and diagnostic equipment.Cancer performance is therefore inextricably linked to the Department’s Elective Care Framework and associated implementation plans.  The Elective Care Framework sets out firm, time-bound proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards, and how we will invest in and transform services to meet the population’s future needs. The Department continues to use a multi-strand approach to tackle lengthy waits. This includes investing in and building core capacity to ensure sustainable service delivery. The approach involves working with clinicians and managers to stabilise and reform services, streamline pathways, and expand workforce skill mix to maximise patient care. The system is striving to improve productivity and efficiency by optimising available resources. Alongside this, and where appropriate, we continue to work with the independent sector to tackle waiting lists, particularly for red flag and time-critical patients.  Details of planned investment by specialty, associated targets, and the expected impact on red flag/cancer waiting times where applicable, can be reviewed within the Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan published at the following link: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/elective-care-framework-restart-recovery-and-redesign

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current average time from red flag referral to cancer diagnosis; and (ii) the current average time from cancer diagnosis to a decision to treat being made, broken down by (a) cancer site; (b) Health and Social Care Trust; and (c) regional list for breast cancer.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for a red flag dermatology referral.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of the reason gambling turnover has fallen by more than three-quarters since 2016, while Betting and Gaming Duty has only fallen by one-eighth since 2015-2016, according to the Gambling Licensing and Industry Data 2024 publication.
answer_text: The two specific datasets in the publication use separate methodologies. The business turnover statistics are for land-based gambling and betting operators only. The published Betting and Gaming Duty statistics include both land-based and remote gambling duty paid, calculated as a proportion of profit rather than turnover. There is no direct correlation between the two.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether the Department for Culture, Media and Sport has requested information on gambling-related harms associated with remote gambling in the North, to inform the Executive receiving a share of the statutory levy on remote gambling operators licensed by the Gambling Commission.
answer_text: During joint discussions with my officials, and officials from the Departments of Health (DoH) and Education, officials from the Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) advised that information on the gambling harm picture in Northern Ireland would be required to inform its considerations. Officials from DoH, which has lead responsibility for gambling related harm, are currently carrying out scoping work to develop a better understanding of how to possibly progress policy on gambling in NI. As part of this, DoH commissioned a policy support paper that contains information on gambling-related harms. DoH officials have recently received this paper and are in the process of considering.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider the Implementation of Drive on left signs, for locations close to airports and ports, to alert visiting tourists to drive on the left.
answer_text: Reported instances of driving on the wrong side of the road at the stated locations are fairly rare. Officials monitor the road network on an ongoing basis and receive information on road traffic collisions from the PSNI on all incidents involving personal injury.  My Department is not aware of any instances reported where driving on the wrong side of the road is cited as a cause of collision and therefore my Department has no plans to install signage at these locations.Advice is provided through NI Direct for visitors driving in the North, including the requirement to drive on the left.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the methodology used to collect data on gambling turnover, in the Gambling Licensing and Industry Data 2024 publication.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the methodology used to collect data on Betting and Gaming Duty, in the Gambling Licensing and Industry Data 2024 publication.
answer_text: Data on betting and gaming duty is taken from the County and regional public sector finance UK publication produced by the Office for National Statistics (ONS). ONS estimates regional Betting and Gaming Duty revenues by apportioning the total UK Betting and Gaming Duty based on the total weekly expenditure on gambling payments from the Living Costs and Food Survey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the contribution of hospice care providers in the delivery of a neighbourhood model approach will be prioritised.
answer_text: As part of a wider HSC reset plan, my Department has recently commenced work to develop a Neighbourhood Health Model, which will see more care delivered in primary and community settings to enable people to stay healthier for longer, reducing the demand for expensive, hospital-led care. Delivering upon this agenda requires new thinking about how, where, and by whom care is delivered.  It will require providers from across all aspects of health and social care, including palliative care, to work in new ways to deliver services to people across Northern Ireland. This will be an important cultural change as well as a planning and commissioning challenge. This work will also include a focus on how the voluntary and community sector can support this agenda, through a collaborative approach that recognises the role of this sector in providing care to people in their own homes and communities to reduce reliance on hospitals and support better management of patients in community settings.  My officials are undertaking an extensive programme of engagement with key stakeholders to ensure all partners understand the role they will have to play in delivering this vision.  As part of this stakeholder engagement, I recently launched a Call for Evidence, seeking examples of neighbourhood initiatives from across Northern Ireland.  The Call for Evidence is available on Citizen Space until 18 November, and will provide hospices with an opportunity to ensure that their contribution and potential is reflected in plans for the Neighbourhood model of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he intends to take within the remainder of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate to ensure the financial stability of the hospice sector.
answer_text: I recognise the important role of hospices in supporting people with palliative and end of life care needs.Current funding arrangements for adult hospice services are based on a 50:50 funding arrangement for inpatient services and also for community-based care.  These funding arrangements are for agreed commissioned services costs, not actual hospice running costs. In 2024/25, the value of funding provided by my Department to hospices for contracted adult services was £9.73million.  This figure does not include funding provided by HSC Trusts for any contract arrangements they have with hospices. As part of the annual review of voluntary and community contracts, the funding envelope to hospices is uplifted through annual increases to the contract value.  In 2025/26, an interim uplift of 2.98% has already been applied to hospice contract payments, increasing the value of hospice contracts held by the Department by £342k. This interim uplift does not reflect the impact of any 2025/26 Agenda for Change pay award which, if agreed, would increase and finalise the interim uplift for the year.    My Department has ongoing engagement with the hospice sector as part of the arrangements in place with them for the delivery of contracted services and works with hospices to understand and resolve any funding concerns identified.  These arrangements provide the opportunity, where possible to do so, to respond to any in-year pressures highlighted where there may be a threat to service sustainability. My Department is currently engaging with each of the hospices to undertake a benchmark review in relation to adult hospice funding to agree the profile of their funding allocations against commissioned services.  The aim is to ensure funding allocated is aligned to the 50:50 commissioning arrangements and to consider the need for any re-balancing in funding in line with existing guidance if appropriate.  My Department has commenced a baseline scoping exercise to consider adult palliative care services.  This will seek to fully understand the performance of existing services across the region and whether they are meeting the palliative and end of life care needs of the populations they currently serve, and those they will need to be prepared to serve in the future.  The exercise will include an updated population based palliative and end of life care needs assessment and will also seek to understand the experience and quality of palliative and end of life care across care settings and in all areas.  The outcome of the scoping exercise will help inform the future planning and commissioning of adult palliative care services, including specialist palliative care services provided by hospices. My Department recognises the valuable role that the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice plays in delivering specialist care to children and young people with palliative and end of life care needs across Northern Ireland.My Department has been engaging with the Northern Ireland Chil

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a part-time 20mph speed limit zone can be installed at St Patrick's Primary School, Rasharkin.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North and are listed on my Department’s website, link; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3.This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.While the Tranche 3 programme focuses on schools on rural roads where the national speed limit applies, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas which would also benefit from a reduction in speed limits. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking and cycling.My Department is also carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including 20mph speed limits in general and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when active travel funding will be available to (i) install footpaths from Rasharkin Village to the GAA grounds on the Dreen Road; (ii) complete the footpath linking Rasharkin and Kilrea, as the existing footpath ends at the junction of the Vow Road; and (iii) complete the remaining one mile of footpath, linking Ballymoney Town and Rasharkin Village.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.Whilst enhancements of this nature contribute towards overall spend on active travel, they are generally progressed through the budgets available for Local Transport Safety Measures. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government, and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need. Proposals for footpaths at these locations have been assessed and whilst it has been confirmed that they have sufficient merit to be added to my Department’s overall list of potential future schemes, regrettably however, when assessed against competing priorities, they did not score highly enough for inclusion in the 2025/26 works programme.  These schemes will, however, remain on our list for future consideration should funding become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether consideration has been given to a review of (i) gritting; and (ii) pedestrian crossing installation, policies.
answer_text: My officials regularly undertake internal reviews of departmental policies to take into consideration lessons learned and detail improvements to procedures.The policy in relation to Winter Service is detailed in RSPPG E022 Winter Service and was last revised and published in June 2024. The policy for the installation of controlled pedestrian crossings is detailed in RSPPG E005 Controlled Crossings: Assessment Procedures which was last revised and published in April 2023.As both policies have been subject to recent review, my Department has no plans to undertake further reviews at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when necessary works at Crabby Hill, on Townhill Road, Rasharkin, will take place, the urgency of which has been reinforced throughout the current traffic diversion, as a result of the closure of the Bann Bridge, Kilrea.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. My Department receives many requests for improvements to the road network.  However, we have been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.The Townhill Road at Crabby Hill has previously been assessed for a minor works improvement scheme.  Regrettably, when assessed against these competing priorities, it did not score highly enough to progress for further consideration. The scheme will, however, remain on our list for future consideration should funding become available.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will review the decision not to install a pedestrian crossing at the junction of Gortahar Road and Finvoy Road, Rasharkin, as well as across the Finvoy Road at the traffic island, to serve pupils attending St. Patrick's Primary School and pedestrian traffic.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.However, my department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need. Regrettably, when assessed against these competing priorities, the proposal for a pedestrian crossing at Gortahar Road did not score highly enough for inclusion in the 2025/26 works programme. The scheme will, however, remain on our list for future consideration should funding become available.I have asked my officials to undertake an assessment of the Finvoy Road and advise you of the outcome of this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Planning Improvement Programme.
answer_text: My Department is working collectively with local government and other stakeholders through the Planning Improvement Programme to deliver meaningful improvements to the planning system in the North. The next phase is now underway, focusing on targeted actions and initiatives that support long-term sustainability and improved performance. This phase focuses on planning authority practices and procedures to identify challenges, blockages, and delays in the planning process. We have been  engaging directly and constructively with all 12 local planning authorities and a broad range of stakeholders to better understand where key improvements should be targeted. New legislation was introduced over the summer, to improve both the Pre-Application Community Consultation and the Pre-Determination Hearing processes.  A review of the classes and thresholds for local, major, and regionally significant development, as part of the review of the Development Management Regulations, is moving forward at pace.  Improving the effectiveness and efficiency of the Local Development Plan process remains a key priority for my Department, and my officials continue to work closely with councils on tasks aimed at streamlining the plan preparation process.Work is progressing on a regional trainee planner scheme, developed in collaboration with councils, Belfast Met, and which has the support of the RTPI. It’s anticipated that several trainee planners will join my Department early next year and similarly for a number of councils.My officials continue to scope challenges and opportunities with councils around cost recovery in the planning system, and a public consultation on short term proposals will be launched soon / has been launched.There have been significant improvements in the average processing time for major applications in 2024/25, this has seen the lowest processing times since the transfer of planning powers in 2015. This trend is continuing with major application processing times meeting the 30-week statutory target in the first quarter of 2025/26.  Response times to statutory consultations are also improving with 77% of responses to statutory consultations submitted on time in 2024/2025.  The planning improvement agenda presents an opportunity and a shared responsibility to ensure NI has a modern, effective planning system that delivers positive outcomes for all citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he can provide any assurances that pupils attending the new Causeway Academy will have their school transport needs met.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport. It informs me that when the arrangements for school sites and year groups are finalised, its Transport Service will engage further with the school and relevant transport providers to confirm transport arrangements for the scheduled school opening in September 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support his Department is providing for the amalgamation of North Coast Integrated College, Dunluce School and Coleraine College, on to a single site.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), as proposer of the Development Proposal to establish the new Causeway Academy, has a statutory obligation to proceed with its implementation. The EA has established a Project Board to take forward the planning for the opening of the new school. The Project Board, the Interim Board of Governors and the Principal Designate are currently finalising the plan for the new school to operate across the existing three school sites from September 2026. The school will operate from the existing sites until it is approved for major capital investment.
