question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to recommence the boiler replacement scheme.
answer_text: The Affordable Warmth Scheme is currently open to applications and is set to run until March 2026.  It includes providing grants for boilers and central heating systems.  The Scheme continues to assist low-income owner-occupiers and households in the private rented sector, with an annual household income of less than £23,000. To make an application to the Affordable Warmth Scheme, customers can contact the NI Energy Advice Service via: 0800 1114455 or email: NIenergyadvice@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on the Domestic Rating Measures consultation.
answer_text: Views were sought on three domestic rate allowances.The 4% early payment discount, which around 160,000 ratepayers take advantage of each year,The maximum capital value cap which applies to almost 8,000 properties valued at more than £400,000,And the 10% landlords’ allowance of rates on rented property which is paid in full by the 30th of September.I have now met with policy officials who have reviewed more than 1,400 responses received as part of the recent consultation on Revenue Raising. I have asked them to move to complete a consultation report on those responses for publication. I will be providing advance copies of that report to the Finance Committee and Ministerial colleagues. I have also asked my officials to integrate the findings of this process with the development of short, medium and long term options that can align with Executive priorities for the remainder of the mandate as these begin to take shape.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much of the £8.1 million funding for the Structural Maintenance Programme for road resurfacing schemes will be spent in the North Antrim constituency.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the road resurfacing schemes to take place in the North Antrim constituency between now and the end of the financial year.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition. While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. Planning and delivery of additional schemes as a result of this funding is on-going. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.The 2023/24 resurfacing programme for the Mid and East Antrim Borough Council and the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council areas were presented to Council members and can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-to-mid-and-east-antrim-council-2023.PDF and https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-council-report-antrim-and-newtownabbey-2023.PDFThe importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's greenways strategy.
answer_text: The Strategic Plan for Greenways identified around 1000km of greenway network across the North, including cross border greenways. Delivery of greenways has generally been led by councils with my Department funding up to 50% of the capital cost of delivering a project. Evidence gathered through engagement with councils highlights that a total of 122km of that network is in place. Acknowledging that progress on delivery of the strategy has been slower than I would like, in 2022 I asked that my Department take a greater role in the delivery of greenways. Officials are currently working closely with councils to better understand their 5-year greenway programme, and to develop an appropriate grant and support framework for the effective delivery of greenways. Future delivery will be dependant upon availability of funding and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on bluetongue disease; and (ii) when he will lift restrictions on importing sheep from Scotland.
answer_text: On 11 November 2023, the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) confirmed a single case of Bluetongue (BTV-3) in a cow in Kent, England.  As of 28 February 2024, there have been 121 individual cases of BTV-3 in England on 71 premises in four counties.  114 of these cases have been in cattle and seven have been in sheep.		As a result of the BTV-3 confirmations in GB ruminant animals, including sheep, cannot move here as the relevant Export Health Certificates (EHC) cannot be signed by GB Official Veterinarians.  The impacted EHCs have been removed by Defra. 		Movements of ruminants from GB to NI can recommence when GB recovers disease free status for BTV.  The minimum period required before disease free status can be granted is two years from the date of the last positive case.		Movements may be permitted sooner should regionalisation for BTV be pursued by Defra and the devolved administrations with approval granted by the European Union.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will reinstate facilitates to allow for a train halt in Dunloy on the Derry to Belfast route, to help increase the use of public transport and sustainable travel.
answer_text: My Department is working towards finalising the All-Island Strategic Rail Review which considers how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation. The Review includes a recommendation to explore the case for developing new stations in the Belfast and Derry regions. However, being at a strategic level, the Review has not considered the location of these stations. Once the Review is published, further work will be required to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support any future investment decisions, including those in relation to the development of any new rail halts along the existing network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority has changed the process for parental referrals for Statutory Assessments.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there are plans to submit an all-island bid to host the Tour De France.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will publish the consultation on a new draft seabird conservation strategy.
answer_text: The Seabird Conservation Strategy is one of several high-profile marine conservation strategies and action plans that I will be considering over the coming months. My intent is to publish the consultation on the draft Seabird Strategy for Northern Ireland during the second half of 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will establish a Nature for Climate Fund to support the delivery of nature-based projects.
answer_text: The funding that will be required for nature-based solutions will not become clear until both the Nature Recovery Strategy and the Climate Action Plan are finalised. Both support nature-based solutions to help us meet multiple environmental objectives, and to deliver on emissions reduction.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he will ensure nature-based projects are prioritised as part of the forthcoming Climate Action Plan.
answer_text: It is a requirement of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 for all departments to have regard to nature-based projects in deciding its policies and proposals for inclusion in the Climate Action Plan. My Department continues to work collaboratively with other Departments to provide guidance and examples of nature-based projects which can help to reduce emissions and bolster climate resilience in Northern Ireland. The draft Climate Action Plan will further outline our approach to nature-based projects.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to introduce a sheep scheme under the Future Agriculture Policy.
answer_text: In January 2023, the Sheep Industry Taskforce produced its report to identify the needs of the sheep sector in Northern Ireland and a proposed package to encourage its development.  I look forward to receiving a briefing on this report to better understand the strategic needs of the sector and will consider deploying levers to meet those needs. Any decision to introduce new policies or programmes will be in the context of budget availability within the ring-fenced farm support funding envelope.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his timeframe to bring a Climate Action Plan to the Assembly.
answer_text: My department is leading the development of Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan, in collaboration with all departments. This Plan will set out Northern Ireland’s approach to delivering the emissions reductions required to achieve our first carbon budget. I intend to seek agreement from Executive colleagues for Northern Ireland’s first three carbon budgets. I will then bring forward draft regulations to the Assembly for its consideration and approval.I am fully committed to delivering a Climate Action Plan that is transformational, evidence-based and informed by stakeholders. The draft Climate Action Plan will need to be agreed by the Executive. The timeframes involved mean that it will not be possible to meet the dates for final publication as set out in the Climate Change Act. However I am determined that we will publish the draft for consultation as soon as possible.The Climate Action Plan will be considered by the DAERA Assembly Committee, and other Committees may also wish to scrutinise their departments’ contributions.As the member will be aware, budgets for the next financial year have not yet been confirmed, however the cost of implementing the Climate Change Act is being considered as part of the ongoing budget exercise for next year.  Work is ongoing to scope and refine these costs. Some of these will fall to DAERA but other departments will bear costs too. Normal budgeting processes will apply and it is also important that the costs and opportunities of addressing climate change are embedded within the economic appraisal processes. Finally, I want to emphasise that these up front costs represent investments in an improved society for future generations and that the cost of delaying action will ultimately be much greater. We must act now and we must act decisively.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new school building for the Mary Queen of Peace Primary School, Glenravel.
answer_text: Mary Queen of Peace Primary School was announced under the Major Capital Works Programme to advance in planning in January 2013.Planning approval for the new school build was granted in April 2021.Outline agreement for land purchase has very recently been agreed, however acquisition of the site cannot be progressed at this time due to the current capital budget position. Procurement of the Building Contractor has also been paused at this time again due to the current capital budget position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Ballycastle Shared Education Campus.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department appointed Heron Brothers in early 2023 as the Design and Build contractor to build the new £70 million campus which will be shared between Ballycastle High School and Cross and Passion College.The contractor is currently undertaking final design work with a view to commencing construction of the modern, state of the art education facilities in spring of this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he plans to commence the further roll out of 20mph speed limit schemes at schools across North Antrim.
answer_text: Part-time 20mph limits near schools are implemented based on assessments considering collision history, speed, traffic volume, infrastructure, and site constraints.  Schools have been assessed in accordance with DfI’s policy ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructureni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070)The program to date has been rolled out in tranches, with the most recent completed in November 2022.  Over the coming weeks, I will be considering my priorities, including the potential for a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme, in the context of the funding that is currently available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed new footpath from Portglenone village along the Gortgole Road towards Bracknamuckley forest and Portglenone GAC grounds.
answer_text: My Department has a footway/cycleway scheme programmed on the Gortgole Road from the entrance of the play park in Portglenone to the entrance of Portglenone GAC grounds on Lovers Lane. Subject to the successful purchase of land, the scheme could start in the financial year 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on work being carried out to address the blue green algae ecological problem impacting Lough Neagh and other water bodies.
answer_text: There are no quick fixes to the occurrence and probable recurrence of blue green algal (BGA) blooms at Lough Neagh. This is a problem that has been decades in the making and it could take decades to resolve.However, I am pleased to confirm that action on the ground has already commenced. For example, my Department, through its College for Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE), is delivering programmes for farmers / land managers at various levels targeted towards Lough Neagh catchment areas. Work on a Small Business Research Initiative scheme to identify viable technical solutions to deal with the immediate effects of BGA within the Lough will also commence shortly. Looking to longer term solutions, my Department has established a cross- departmental Water Quality Steering Group (WQSG) which is currently finalising a draft report with a set of evidenced-based recommendations to tackle BGA and secure longer-term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland. Although I expect to bring these recommendations to the Executive for discussion and consideration shortly, I would stress that Lough Neagh cannot be seen in isolation from the natural environment more generally and we cannot tackle its problems in isolation either. I look forward to working with the Executive, the Assembly and indeed all stakeholders in finding solutions to address this complex issue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to increase the provision of electric vehicle charging points in Ballymena.
answer_text: The current electric vehicle public charge point network is owned by the Electricity Supply Board (ESB) and is operated on a commercial basis. ESB announced in November 2021 that it had been awarded £3.27 million from the Levelling Up Fund to expand and enhance the EV charging network. ESB can be contacted directly regarding its plans for its charge point infrastructure. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure. I am pleased that my Department has been able to support the FASTER Project, funded through the INTERREG VA Territorial Co-operation Programme which is managed by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB). The project has received funding to install a total of 73 EV Rapid charging points across the island of Ireland and the West of Scotland by 31 May 2023. It is expected that 20-30 of these will be located in the North.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). A local council consortium, led by Derry City and Strabane District Council, submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust which manages the fund on behalf of OZEV on 30 March 2022. Derry City and Strabane District council is best placed to advise about the application. My Department is represented, in an advisory capacity, on the ORCS council consortium. My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what urgent steps she is taking to resolve and address the issues with the NI Supply Teacher Register to ensure payment to teachers.
answer_text: The Department funds the Education Authority to administer the NI Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) which is effectively a booking system enabling schools to source and engage professionally qualified teachers who have undergone a range of pre-employment checks.  There have been a number of issues regarding the implementation of a new NISTR system and the timely payment of temporary teachers. The Department and the EA continue to work together intensively, alongside stakeholder groups, to ensure that immediate steps are taken to ensure staff are paid for their valuable service.  In the current exceptional circumstances, particularly in light of the cost-of-living crisis, the Department has asked the EA to process interim emergency payments to all teachers impacted as a matter of urgency.Due to administrative errors in extracting the file and uploading it to the payroll system; 309 temporary and supply teachers received only partial salary on 18 October for bookings that were approved by schools and should have been paid under normal circumstances.  The EA has sincerely apologised for this error and is processing interim emergency payments to reach these teachers by 25 October.The other factor that has caused problems in the timely payment of temporary teachers relates to the fact that the appropriate approval processes at school level were not completed in line with the payroll deadline.  This prevented payment being made on the planned date for an estimated 300 teachers.  Incorrect data input in respect of hours worked also appears to have featured in the sign-off process.  The EA is contacting all relevant schools to seek confirmation of attendance, check period of service and obtain other necessary sign-off information to enable further interim emergency payments to pursued.  In respect of the system issues, a number of fixes have been applied to address identified bugs and provide additional functionality for example, in line with strong school user feedback, a search by teacher name or TR number was added on 22 September.  Work continues to make further improvements and changes to system logic based on user feedback.  Planned additional features will continue to be added throughout the developmental phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider using the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme to officially record and document the names of fields.
answer_text: The DAERA online services portal is currently used for registrations into the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS). This is a voluntary scheme and registrations for the first year of its operation are due to close on 31 August 2022. The registration process includes verifying fields to be soil sampled within a digitised map and is a similar process to completing a Single Application. The design of the portal was built to make the registration process as simple as possible for farmers to register their fields by field number.The SNHS data is fed from the field data held on the Land Parcel Identification System (LPIS) which is used for the purpose of administering payments to farm businesses.  There are approximately 735,000 fields with a unique field ID number. Approximately 7,000 field numbers a year need amended where fields are split, merged or a change in use.  I note the work being done in the Republic of Ireland, within the Kilkenny Field Name Recording Project, supported by local councils and Creative Ireland. The focus of the data collection within the SNHS is specifically on field usage both now and in the future and not field ownership which we do not hold details on. Therefore, perhaps Land Registry might be a more appropriate vehicle for collection of field name data. No doubt this would require a cost benefit analysis exercise to determine if a field name collection project, would be a cost effective and appropriate use of public funding.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide copies of his correspondence with the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, or other DEFRA officials, regarding transposing of the EU Single Use Plastics Directive into law in this jurisdiction.
answer_text: It would not be appropriate for me to make public ministerial communications, covering a range of matters, concerning ongoing policy development. However, I can advise that I confirmed to the Secretary of State in writing on two occasions and verbally at various meetings that the UK Government had made the agreement in respect of single-use plastics with the EU and therefore this was a matter for the UK Government. I have also asked for confirmation of UK Government plans for transposition so that an update could be provided to NI businesses.The UK Government has since recognised deficiencies in the NI Protocol and is now seeking to rectify these either through negotiation with the EU or through domestic legislation.Meanwhile Scottish and Welsh Government plans to introduce legislation on single-use plastics have been found to be inconsistent with the UK Internal Market Act (IMA). A legal exclusion to the IMA for certain single-use plastics is currently being progressed and there also remains an ongoing legal case by the Welsh Government on the powers of the IMA.Until these deficiencies with the Protocol and the internal UK legal issues have been thoroughly addressed it would be prudent to keep on hold any further work on single-use plastics legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the restrictions for overnight parking by caravanettes and caravans on (i) public roads; and (ii) Carrickmore Road, Ballycastle.
answer_text: The following legislation applies:Article 91 of The Roads (NI) Order 1993 (1993 No. 3160 (N.I. 15)) provides that any person who without reasonable excuse encamps or maintains an encampment on a road including with a caravan shall be guilty of an offence.Regulation 27 of the Road Vehicles Lighting Regulations (NI) 2000 (SR 200 No.169), sets out requirements regarding the use of front and rear position lamps, rear registration plate lamps, side marker lamps and end-outline marker lamps which apply to vehicles parking on roads during the hours between sunset and sunrise.  This would also apply to caravans.Regulation 119 of the Motor Vehicles (Construction and Use) Regulations (NI) 1999 (SR 1999 No.454), concerns the unnecessary obstruction of a road by a motor vehicle or a trailer.With regard to overnight parking on the Carrickmore Road, Ballycastle, there is no specific prohibition on parking along any part of the road.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.
